segments.text: The word Anukirtaya is very significant. Anukirtaya means to follow the description, not to create a concocted mental description, but to follow.

So, Shaunaka Rishi requested Suta Goswami to describe what he had actually heard from his spiritual master, Shukadeva Goswami, about the transcendental pastimes of the Lord manifested by his internal potency.
Bhagwan, the supreme personality of Godhead, has no material body, but he can assume any kind of body by his supreme will. That is made possible by his internal potency.
So, these are the three things.

These are the words spoken by Shaunaka Rishi, who is asking Suta Goswami, please describe all the activities and pastimes of the personality of Godhead, who is full of self-desire, and who assumes all these activities by his internal potency.
So, Prabhupada mentions, Anukirtaya means to follow the description, and not to create a concocted mental description, but to follow.

potency.
He has innumerable, inconceivable potencies, and it's all possible, smaller than the smallest, bigger than the biggest, finer than the finest, older than the oldest.
So these are all, once we understand who the Supreme Lord is, how he's having innumerable, unlimited potencies, powers, abilities, then when we hear these pastimes, they are not fiction for us.
In fact, our faith in the Lord gets reinforced.
Our conviction that the Lord is so great, he can do anything and everything, gets reiterated when we hear such pastimes, rather than we doubt.

When I was young, maybe three, four, four years, four, five years, so when we would go to watch some, we were staying in a township, so invariably on a Saturday or Sunday, they would screen some movies.
Those days, VCRs and all that were not there, so those film roles.
So invariably.
In the colony, the township, on weekends, they would screen such movies.
And whenever I would watch, imagine five years old, boy, not have complete understanding of the world.
Whenever I would watch the movie, I clearly remember, invariably that movie would have some songs, hero and heroine, on top of the mountains, and then suddenly heroes start singing.
And then, so I used to wonder, on top of the mountain, hero is singing, how his voice is coming from, how this orchestra, sound, band, everything is coming.
He is on top of a mountain, a small child, he will think like that.
Now, grown-ups also, it doesn't strike them, lip-syncing and all those things, top of a mountain, distant.
Sometime we do video recording, we have to really struggle hard.
So that sound comes properly.
Wind is blowing, and then he's in a deep in a forest, he's singing, and along with that, a band is there, some guitar is there, some, you know, all that musical instruments are there.
So I used to wonder how this musical sound is coming.
Actually, if we see a shooting of a movie, it's all to shoot one scene, cut, cut, retake, cut, cut, and finally all this cut, cut, retakes.
They all start.
They're all stitched together, and, you know, experts sit together and weave it together, and finally the movie comes out, and it brings about a great illusion.
We get absorbed in the plot.

You know, it's all disjointed scenes.
It doesn't make any sense, but once it is stitched together, it creates an illusion.
We get so absorbed in the plot, in the scene.

So, Bhagavatam says, compares crows and the swans.
Generally, we find crows where garbage is there, filth is there, some dead body is there.
And where do we find swans?
Where there is pristine, pure, clear water.
Although Bhagavatam is so sublime, such wonderful pastimes of the Lord are there, but people have no interest in them.
If we see a typical Sunday morning, although it's open for everyone, people can come, hear Bhagavatam, have prasadam, have darshan, but we see 200, 250 people.
The problem is that people don't, people have a mentality of a crow.
And people have a basic, crowlike consciousness, where filth, garbage, all those things captures their attention.
Bhagavatam starts with paramo nirmatsaranam satam.
So, this Bhagavatam, it's meant for people who are paramo nirmatsaranam satam, non-envious.

So Prabhupada says it is not some, the Lord's pastimes which are manifested by his internal potency is not some concoction, it is enacted by his supreme will and made possible by his internal potency.

We were talking about the crows and the swans. We also understand from Bhagavatam, from Bhagavad Gita that there is an energy of the Lord, illusory energy of the Lord which Krishna, it's mamamaya dhurateya and the function of illusory energy is to keep us in illusion.
It's agent of the Lord. It's energy of the Lord.
It is doing good.
It is doing the thankless job of keeping us in illusion.
Just like Durga Devi. Durga is called fort. Durga, very difficult to surmount, to come out of that fort. We are captive in that fort, Durga.
So illusory energy, why it is? Because we want to enjoy, independent of Krishna.
We don't want to surrender to Krishna. We don't want to, ichcha, dvesha, samutthena, dvandamohena, bharata.
We want, we have different desires, ichcha and dvesha, envy of the Lord. So we have been sent here to try it out.
And such souls who are rebellious of the Lord, till they get purified,
they shouldn't be purified.
They shouldn't be disturbing the Lord and His devotees and His wonderful pastimes.
So this illusory energy keeps them within this material world.
And only when we express our desires,
maam eva ye prapadyante, purify, tapo divyam shudhyet brahma saukhyam tu anantam,
when we start purifying our life, expressing our desires by doing devotional service that Krishna,
I want you, I want to serve you, enough of service of my senses, mind, maya,
then her grip on us starts diminishing.
This is sum and substance of what we are doing, devotional service.
maam eva ye prapadyante, mayam etam tarantit.
Krishna, the maya energy, which is energy of the Lord, releases the grip on us.
And when the Lord says, I want you, I want you, I want to serve you, enough of service of my senses, mind, maya, then her grip on us starts diminishing.
When the grip is completely loosened, then we go back to Krishna.
So there is a very nice article in Christianity, there is some synonym for this maya, or illusionary, it is also called Saturn.

you are contacting the Lord, chanting the holy names.

I don't think we can,

Tempt them to spend, spend, spend and borrow, borrow and borrow. Many times people who are working in the corporate world, they have four, five credit cards or even more sometimes.
And why they get it? Because they're earning some salaries or the banks offer because that's the way they make money by giving credit and on that interest.
So initially when you get the credit card, you feel good because you can go to the shop, you can buy whatever you want, swipe the card and all that thing.
But then it so happens that many times because senses are untamed, uncontrolled, unlimited desires, we get tempted.
We also as devotees, many times we get tempted and this today's world, there's a lot of allurements.
So invariably people overspend. So every credit card has some limit. You reach that limit and then you have to pay the money till you further use it, for you to use further.
So if they have four, five credit cards, they, you know, touch the limits of all the cards.
And they get into a debt cycle. The first day of the month when the salary comes, the entire salary practically goes in paying for the credit card bills and things like that.
Because they overspend. So spend, spend, spend, borrow, borrow, borrow.

Give them such celebrations that distract them from teaching them.
